personSummary.value: Former home sewist who feels most settled when her red cardigan is visible and morning care is unhurried. Responds well to folding towels, one-step tasks, lavender scent, and quiet explanations before medication. Family says sewing-table routines and clean towels were familiar daily anchors. Recent morning care notes show distress when the cardigan is missing or care is rushed.

citations.quote: red cardigan both help her feel ready for the day
folded towels with staff after lunch

value.description: Morning uneasiness is linked to the red cardigan being out of sight.

citations.quote: became uneasy when it was not on the chair

value.description: Rushed morning care recently led to tearfulness and repeated searching for the cardigan.

citations.quote: resident became tearful and asked twice where her cardigan was

value: Place the red cardigan where she can see it before morning care.
Offer a simple towel-folding task after lunch or during transitions.
Use lavender hand cream and explain each step in a quiet voice.

citations.quote: explained each step in a quiet voice

value: Red cardigan missing from the chair or bed during morning care.
Rushed room activity nearby before she understands the plan.

citations.quote: when it was not on the chair

value.approach: Find the red cardigan first, confirm the plan, and begin with one slow step.

citations.quote: found the cardigan, and restarted care slowly

value.approach: Invite her to fold towels with staff and offer lavender hand cream afterward.

citations.quote: folded towels with staff after lunch

handoffBrief.value: Haruko Tanaka settles best when her red cardigan is visible, care starts slowly, and staff offer towel-folding or lavender cues; watch morning distress if the cardigan is missing or nearby activity feels rushed.

value.claim: Watch whether cardigan-related distress appears only in the morning or during other care transitions.

citations.quote: asked twice where her cardigan was
